title: Poetry Review: ‘Wellwater,’ by Karen Solie
description: The Canadian poet Karen Solie balances environmental concerns with hope and deadpan wit.
hook: A Canadian poet writes about dying ecosystems with deadpan wit—environmental despair that somehow feels like dark comedy.

title: Interview: Tony Leung on ‘Silent Friend’ and Expanding His Understanding of Consciousness
description:
The acting legend discusses how he’s been changed by connecting to the consciousness of plants.

The post
hook: Tony Leung on how acting expands consciousness itself—a conversation about bodies, presence, and what performance actually does to the mind.

title: The Grandmothers Who Become Mothers Again
description: In “Mawmaw,” the photographer Anthony Wilson pays tribute to West Virginia women who, after one tragedy or another, care for their children’s children.
hook: West Virginia grandmothers raising their grandchildren: a photographer's intimate look at love when tragedy rewrites family structure.

title: The rise of the literary nepo baby? The children of famous novelists on following in their parents’ footsteps
description:
From Naomi Ishiguro to Jess Atwood Gibson, more children of high profile writers are becoming authors themselves. Parents and their literary offspring discuss the pressures of measuring up

Martin Amis liked to observe that the unusual position he and Kingsley Amis held – father-and-son novelists – was a historical anomaly, a “literary curiosity”. But it was not unique: Alexandre Dumas père and fils, Fanny and Anthony Trollope, and Arthur and Evelyn Waugh had all come before them.
hook: When your parent is a famous novelist, you're either following their footsteps or running from their shadow—either way, the weight is unbearable.

title: Place presents itself to you in fragments: Ivan Vladislavić and Jeanne-Marie Jackson
description:
“The notion of the quintessential Johannesburg book might be quite a cheeky one. There are so many Johannesburgs.”

The post Place presents itself to you in fragments: Ivan Vladislavić and Jeanne-Marie Jackson appeared first on Public Books.
hook: Ivan Vladislavić on how Johannesburg refuses to cohere into one story—fragments reveal more truth than any coherent narrative could.

title: Machines To Listen For Us: How the Microphone Changed the Nature of Nosiness
description:
In an exclusive extract from his new book, Microphone, comedian Ralph Jones looks at the history of wiretaps, hidden mics and baby monitors

Ralph performing comedy at the microphone

Just as the microphone didn’t invent singing, it didn’t invent eavesdropping. We have always listened in to other people’s conversations because we have always been
hook: The microphone didn't just record sound; it made eavesdropping, surveillance, and nosiness technologically possible. Everything changed.

title: What Adults Lose When They Put Down Children’s Books
description: Grown-ups who dismiss literature for kids aren’t just snobbish—they’re missing out.
hook: Adults who dismiss children's literature are protecting themselves from something—and missing what actually matters about how stories shape us.
